content_en:
Truthfulness (sidq) occupies a supreme position in the Islamic ethical hierarchy — the Prophet ﷺ was known as 'al-Amin' (the Trustworthy) and 'al-Sadiq' (the Truthful) by his own community before his prophethood, and the Quran elevated truthfulness to the level of a defining characteristic of the believers: 'O you who believe, fear Allah and be with the truthful' (Quran 9:119). Al-Nawawi's chapter on sidq in Riyadh al-Salihin brings together the most compelling prophetic hadiths on this virtue and its opposite.

The foundational hadith of this chapter is one of the most structurally powerful statements in the Sunnah, building an entire moral universe in a few sentences: 'Truthfulness leads to righteousness, and righteousness leads to Paradise. A man keeps on telling the truth until he becomes a truthful person. Falsehood leads to wickedness, and wickedness leads to Hellfire. A man may keep on telling lies until he is written before Allah as a liar.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This narration establishes truthfulness not merely as a single virtuous act but as the first link in a causal chain that terminates in Paradise — and falsehood as the first link in a chain that terminates in Hellfire. Character is built through repeated choices, and the choice of truth, made consistently, creates a truthful person.

Al-Nawawi includes the hadith about the three signs of the hypocrite (munafiq): 'When he speaks, he lies; when he makes a promise, he breaks it; and when he is given a trust, he betrays it.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This hadith is not limited to theological hypocrisy but describes a moral pattern — a person who routinely practices these three things has hypocritical character, regardless of the sincerity of their formal religious affiliation. Truth in speech, fidelity to promises, and trustworthiness in what is entrusted are the markers of genuine Muslim character.

The chapter also includes the Prophet's ﷺ guidance on the subtle forms of dishonesty that believers must avoid. The hadith recorded by Abu Dawud describes a woman calling her child with the promise of something: 'Come here and I will give you something' — and the Prophet ﷺ asked: 'What do you intend to give him?' When she said dates, he said: 'If you had not given him something, a lie would have been recorded against you.' This narration demonstrates the Prophet's ﷺ extraordinary scrupulousness regarding even the most casual and unintentional forms of falsehood.

Al-Nawawi concludes this chapter with the understanding that truthfulness in speech is inseparable from truthfulness of character — sidq al-hal, the alignment of the outward presentation of oneself with one's actual inner reality. The believer's truthfulness encompasses their words, their promises, their representations of themselves and others, and the correspondence between their public and private selves. The highest form of sidq is what the Sufis call sidq al-maqam — the authenticity of one's spiritual station, where what one claims to be in their religious life genuinely corresponds to what one has actually achieved through sustained effort and divine grace.
